metadata.summary: Systematically organize, label, and structure navigation systems so users find what they need without the content matching internal org structures.

content.title: Information Architecture - Framework
content.summary: Information architecture (IA) is the structural design of shared information environments. It involves organizing, labeling, and creating navigation systems that help users find and understand information. A robust IA framework prevents low findability, user frustration, increased support costs, and poor scalability as content grows.

why.p: Without proper IA, users cannot find what they need. Content organization matches the company, not users. Navigation is confusing and inconsistent. Search becomes the only way to find anything. Adding new content breaks existing structure. Different teams organize content differently.

Optimizing individual touchpoints without seeing the full information landscape creates local maxima: one screen improves while handoffs between sections or channels get worse. Information architecture forces cross-functional visibility into the complete structure and surface friction that no single team owns.

list.item: At the start of a product or site build, before any navigation or page structure is committed to code.
When users consistently report they cannot find things and analytics confirm high search usage and low direct navigation.
During a content migration or redesign where the existing structure needs to be evaluated and reorganized.
When adding a new content type or feature to an existing product — to validate it fits the current IA before creating a one-off page.
When onboarding stakeholders who need to understand the product's structural logic before contributing content.
For single-purpose tools with fewer than 10 distinct screens — a simple user flow diagram suffices.
When the product's navigation is already well-validated by tree testing and usability data — redo IA only when evidence warrants it.
As the first step in a research process; IA should follow user research (card sorting, interviews), not precede it.
When the team has no authority over navigation or content organization (e.g., working inside a locked CMS template).

how-to.step: Research: Understand the content (content inventory, content types and relationships, growth projections), understand users (user research, mental models, common tasks and goals), and understand business (goals, key content priorities, governance needs).
Define Strategy: Based on research, determine the primary user tasks, most important content, how users will expect to find things, and how the structure will scale.

Create Organization Schemes: Select among Topic (by subject matter, for content sites), Task (by user goal, for tool/app UIs), Audience (by user type, for multi-audience sites), Alphabetical (A-Z order, for directories and glossaries), Chronological (by date, for news and blogs), and Geographic (by location, for location-based services).
Define Taxonomy: Create consistent naming with categories (top-level groups), subcategories (nested groups), tags (cross-cutting labels), and metadata (structured attributes).

Design Navigation: Design global navigation (appears on every page), local navigation (within a section), contextual navigation (related content links), utility navigation (account, help, settings), footer navigation (secondary links, legal), and breadcrumbs (location in hierarchy).
Validate: Test with users via card sorting (organization), tree testing (findability), and usability testing (real use).

section.name: ia-components
section.p: Information architecture has four core components:

rule.statement: Organization: How content is grouped into categories and sections, reflecting either subject matter (topic-based), user goals (task-based), or user type (audience-based).
rule.rationale: The organization scheme is the foundation for all downstream navigation design. Choosing the wrong scheme — such as org-chart navigation that matches internal structure instead of user mental models — undermines findability from the start.

rule.statement: Labeling: How content is named in menus, headings, and navigation items. Labels must be plain language that users recognize, not internal jargon.
rule.rationale: Labels are the primary interface between user intent and content. Jargon labels or generic terms like "Resources" and "Solutions" force users to search rather than browse because they cannot predict what is behind each label.

rule.statement: Navigation: How users move through the information space. Navigation types include global (every page), local (within sections), contextual (related items), utility (account, help), and footer (secondary and legal).
rule.rationale: Different navigation types serve different user intents. Mixing all navigation into a single mega-menu or removing navigation types entirely creates cognitive overload and hidden content.

rule.statement: Search: How users query the information space when browsing fails. Search requires metadata (tags, descriptions) to work well and should be complemented by filters and facets.
rule.rationale: Search is a safety net, not a primary navigation method. When users resort to search, it signals that browsing structure is insufficient. Improve structure first; search optimizations are secondary.

section.name: ia-principles
section.p: Every information architecture should be evaluated against four principles:

rule.statement: Findability: Users can locate what they need through navigation, search, or context without exceeding three clicks or requiring prior knowledge of taxonomy terms.
rule.rationale: Findability is the core promise of IA. If users cannot find content after clicking three times or searching, the IA has failed. Five-level hierarchies and generic labels fail this test.

rule.statement: Understandability: Labels make sense to users without domain expertise. Labels must be tested with representative users, not assumed to be clear.
rule.rationale: Teams working inside a domain lose perspective on what is obvious and what is jargon. First-click testing with target users reveals assumptions about label clarity.

rule.statement: Scalability: The structure accommodates growth in content volume and new content types without requiring wholesale reorganization.
rule.rationale: Taxonomies built for 50 pages of content often break when reaching 500 pages. Anticipate growth and design flexibility — allow new content to fit into existing categories or establish clear criteria for new top-level categories.

rule.statement: Flexibility: Content can live in multiple contexts — appear in more than one category, belong to multiple tags, or be surfaced in different navigation paths depending on user mental model.
rule.rationale: Rigid tree structures force content into a single parent, creating navigation paths that do not match some users' mental models. Faceted navigation and cross-category tagging reduce this friction.

antipattern.description: Org chart navigation — using internal reporting structure (Product, Engineering, Sales, Marketing sections) instead of user mental models (Features, Documentation, Community, Support).
antipattern.reason: Org-chart navigation mirrors how the company is structured, not how users think about the product. Users do not know which internal team owns a feature; they search by feature or task.

antipattern.description: Too deep hierarchy — more than 3-4 levels in navigation.
antipattern.reason: Beyond 3 levels, users lose context of where they are and retreat to search. Each level adds a decision point and a chance for wrong turns.

antipattern.description: Too many choices — more than 7 items per navigation level (Miller's Law).
antipattern.reason: Humans cannot comfortably hold more than 7 items in working memory while making a choice. Above 7 items, navigation becomes a search task in itself.

antipattern.description: Jargon labels — using internal terminology (API Keys, Service Tiers, Webhook Integrations) in public navigation without translation.
antipattern.reason: Users working with the product understand jargon through repeated exposure, but first-time visitors and occasional users will not recognize terms. Test labels with people outside your company.

antipattern.description: No testing — assumptions about navigation not validated with users.
antipattern.reason: Even experienced designers misjudge label clarity and category groupings. Card sorting and tree testing reveal mismatches; assumptions should not drive IA.

section.name: ia-artifacts
section.p: IA work produces several deliverables, each serving a specific purpose at different project stages:

rule.statement: Sitemap (throughout project): A visual hierarchy showing all pages/sections and how they relate. Used for planning, communication, and coverage checks.
rule.rationale: Sitemaps are the lingua franca between stakeholders. A documented sitemap prevents scope creep and ensures all parties see the same structure.

rule.statement: Taxonomy document (early stage): A living reference of all categories, subcategories, tags, and naming conventions. Governs where new content will be placed.
rule.rationale: Without a taxonomy document, teams will interpret categories differently and add conflicting content types. A documented taxonomy prevents inconsistency at scale.

rule.statement: Navigation specification (mid to late stage): Detailed design of global, local, contextual, and utility navigation, including label text, visual hierarchy, and responsive behavior.
rule.rationale: Navigation design bridges IA (what exists and how it relates) from visual design (how it is presented). Specs prevent designers from inventing navigation patterns that contradict the IA.

rule.statement: Page inventory or content table (mid stage): A spreadsheet or database listing every page/feature with URL, owner, template type, priority, and metadata. Used to track coverage and manage updates.
rule.rationale: Large sites drift into unmaintained content. An inventory with an owner column ensures someone is responsible for each page and makes it visible when content is deprecated but not removed.

rule.statement: Wireframes and mockups (late stage): Layout sketches showing how navigation elements appear in context with content, often annotated with interaction notes.
rule.rationale: Wireframes ground the IA in real layout constraints — a 7-item mega-menu might not fit responsively on mobile. Early wireframe review catches infeasible structures.

section.name: when-ia-fails-limitations
section.p: Information architecture has real limitations and common failure modes that teams should understand before investing heavily in IA work:

antipattern.description: IA designed without user input reflects the organization's mental model, not users' — org-chart navigation is the most common failure.
antipattern.reason: Internal teams lose perspective on their own jargon. Org-chart navigation matches internal reporting structure, not user mental models of the product. Users do not think "engineering," "product," "sales" — they think about their jobs and the tools that solve them.

antipattern.description: Tree testing validates findability of a specific label structure but not the navigation experience (visual design, microcopy, discoverability).
antipattern.reason: Tree testing strips visual design, making all navigation equally visible. In practice, visual design, color, proximity, and font size affect findability dramatically. A label that scores well in tree testing may still fail in visual UI if it is de-emphasized or buried.

antipattern.description: Deep hierarchies (5+ levels) test well in tree testing but fail in practice because users lose context of where they are.
antipattern.reason: Tree testing asks discrete questions ("find this item") without context. Real navigation requires users to hold their location in the hierarchy in memory. Five levels exceeds working memory; users cannot remember where they are and give up.

rule.statement: IA framework covers structure and labeling; it does not address search quality, which becomes the primary navigation for large content sets (500+ pages).
rule.rationale: Beyond 500 pages, taxonomy inevitably breaks — too many edge cases and ambiguous categorizations. Search and facets become necessary. A good IA provides the metadata for search (tags, descriptions); it does not replace search.

rule.statement: Scalability assumptions built into the taxonomy break when content volume grows faster than planned.
rule.rationale: A 50-page taxonomy with clearly distinct categories may have been well-designed. At 200 pages, new content does not fit existing categories and forces reorganization. At 500+ pages, the entire structure is contested. Anticipate 10x growth and design flexibility in.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents can accelerate IA work at several stages: generating first-pass structures from a content inventory, evaluating existing sitemaps against IA principles, flagging taxonomy maintenance issues, and producing quarterly health reports from page analytics. A human conducts user research, makes final structural decisions, and validates generated work.

rule.statement: An agent can generate a first-pass IA structure from a content inventory or product description, evaluate an existing sitemap against IA principles (findability, understandability, scalability, flexibility), and produce tree testing questions from a proposed IA.
rule.rationale: Agents excel at pattern matching and generating plausible structures quickly. The output is a starting point for human review, not final IA. Validation against user research findings, card sorting results, and tree testing is mandatory before implementation.

rule.statement: For ongoing governance, an agent can flag when new content added to a CMS does not fit existing categories, suggest taxonomy placement, and produce a quarterly IA health report from page analytics.
rule.rationale: Manual governance scales poorly. An agent reviewing new content submissions against a taxonomy reference can catch misclassifications before they ship, maintaining consistency.

rule.statement: Agents default to topic-based organization schemes; if the product needs task-based or audience-based IA, specify this explicitly — the default will not match.
rule.rationale: Topic-based organization (Products, Services, Resources) is the most common pattern in training data. Agents will reproduce this pattern by default unless explicitly instructed otherwise. Provide examples of task-based (Get Started, Build, Deploy, Monitor) or audience-based (For Teams, For Enterprises, For Non-Profits) organization in the prompt.

rule.statement: Agent-generated category labels often include marketing language ("Solutions", "Resources") that users do not recognize as navigation targets; instruct the agent to flag these.
rule.rationale: LLMs optimize for marketing polish, not user-language clarity. Explicit instructions to flag vague or jargon labels help catch these in review before they reach the product.

rule.statement: LLMs cannot evaluate label clarity without knowing the target user's vocabulary; provide a glossary of user terms vs. internal terms in the prompt.
rule.rationale: What is plain language to one user segment (IT Ops) is jargon to another (SMB sales rep). Supply a vocabulary reference so the agent can evaluate label choices against your specific audience.

rule.statement: Agent structural suggestions are based on training data patterns (common SaaS, e-commerce, content site structures); niche domains may require significantly different IA patterns.
rule.rationale: Agents will default to patterns they have seen thousands of times in training. Niche domains (B2B scientific software, financial regulatory content, internal tools) may need custom patterns. Provide examples of similar domains or the desired structure.

rule.statement: Do not use agent-generated IA as input to tree testing without human review; poorly structured agent IA will produce misleading tree test results.
rule.rationale: If the agent's structure is fundamentally flawed, the tree test will show that the flawed structure is also hard to search — confirming the agent's bias. Human review first to catch structural problems before research.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: IA projects succeed when teams follow a few key practices:

rule.statement: Conduct card sorting before finalizing category labels; generate the IA structure from user groupings, then validate with tree testing before committing to code.
rule.rationale: Avoid the sunk-cost trap of building an IA in the abstract and then testing it. Card sort with target users first; their groupings are ground truth. Then design the IA around what users actually do.

rule.statement: Navigation labels should be in the user's vocabulary, not the organization's; test labels with a simple first-click test if card sorting is not feasible.
rule.rationale: A label that makes perfect sense internally ("Services Portal") may be opaque to users ("Features I can use without leaving the product"). Five-minute first-click test with 3 external people catches this before design and development.

rule.statement: Limit navigation depth to 3 levels maximum for task-based UIs; content-heavy sites can justify 4 but rarely 5.
rule.rationale: Each level of navigation adds a decision point and a chance for users to get lost. Task-based UIs (dashboards, tools) should prioritize directness; 3 levels suffices. Content sites (news, documentation) may need 4. Beyond 4 is increasingly rare and needs strong justification.

rule.statement: Separate the IA structure from the visual navigation design — the structure (what exists and how it relates) is separate from the UI pattern (how it is presented).
rule.rationale: Good structure can be poorly presented (bad visual design, bad labeling, bad layout), and mediocre structure can be presented well. Solve the structure problem first via IA; then solve the presentation problem via UI/UX design. Conflating the two produces inconsistent work.

rule.statement: Assign a taxonomy owner with authority to approve new categories; without governance, taxonomies become internally inconsistent within 6–12 months.
rule.rationale: As teams add content independently, each interprets categories slightly differently. An owner with authority to approve and enforce standards prevents category drift and keeps navigation consistent over time.

rule.statement: Review the IA annually against analytics: pages with no traffic despite good placement indicate label or discoverability problems; pages with high exit rates despite being the right destination indicate content problems, not IA problems.
rule.rationale: Analytics separate IA failures (wrong structure, unclear labels) from content failures (bad/irrelevant content). A well-placed page with no traffic usually has a labeling problem. A high-traffic page with high exit rates usually has a content problem. The distinction guides whether to fix IA or content.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Several command-line tools accelerate IA documentation and analysis for large content sets:

rule.statement: Use `tree` to visualize directory-tree IA in markdown docs (built-in on Linux; `brew install tree` on macOS).
rule.rationale: The `tree` command generates an instant visual hierarchy of a directory structure, useful for documenting file-based or folder-based IA (documentation sites, content libraries).

rule.statement: Use `csvkit` + `sqlite3` to query and filter large page inventories for IA analysis (`pip install csvkit` / csvkit.readthedocs.io).
rule.rationale: As page inventories exceed 100 rows, manual spreadsheet filtering becomes error-prone. csvkit allows piping page inventory CSVs through SQL queries, sorting by category, finding orphaned pages, and flagging classification inconsistencies programmatically.

rule.statement: Use `markdown-toc` to auto-generate navigable table of contents for large IA documents (`npm i -g markdown-toc` / github.com/jonschlinkert/markdown-toc).
rule.rationale: Large IA documents (20+ pages) need a table of contents for navigation. markdown-toc auto-generates one from heading levels, preventing manual TOC drift.

section.name: services-and-saas-tools
section.p: Several SaaS services and open-source tools support different stages of IA work:

rule.statement: Optimal Workshop (Treejack for tree testing, OptimalSort for card sorting): Purpose-built IA research tools with partial API support for exporting results to agents for analysis.
rule.rationale: Optimal Workshop is the standard for card sorting and tree testing in UX practice. API export allows agents to synthesize results into a draft IA structure, accelerating the synthesis step.

rule.statement: Miro: Collaborative IA mapping with REST API for board creation and card management.
rule.rationale: Miro enables real-time cross-functional IA mapping workshops. API support allows agents to create boards and populate them from a content inventory, reducing manual diagram work.

rule.statement: Airtable: Taxonomy and content inventory management with full REST API CRUD support.
rule.rationale: Airtable's API allows agents to query and update taxonomies programmatically, maintain page inventories, and enforce governance rules (e.g., flag new content that does not fit existing categories).

rule.statement: Contentful / Headless CMS: Structured content with IA reflected in content model schema. Full REST API + GraphQL for querying content and structure.
rule.rationale: Headless CMS tools embed the IA into the content model itself. Agents can query the schema to validate that new content types fit the designed structure and propose taxonomy updates when they don't.
